402	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
403	
404	\begin{lemma}
405	
406	\leanok
407	[{\v C}ech acyclicity on affines]
408	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
409	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
410	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
411	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG (affine vanishing);
412	  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
413	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) and Lemma
414	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines)
415	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52 and L145--155).
416	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
417	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
418	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
419	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
420	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
421	  % all $p > 0$."
422	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
423	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
424	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
425	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
426	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
427	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
428	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
429	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
430	  vanishing) and Stacks, Tag 02KG
431	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
432	  affines).}
433	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
434	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
435	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
436	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
437	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) has vanishing cohomology
438	  in all positive degrees:
439	  \[
440	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
441	  \]
442	  and consequently \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
443	\end{lemma}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
446	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
447	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
448	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
449	% ...
450	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
451	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
452	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
453	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
454	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
455	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
456	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
457	% Let us define a homotopy
458	% $$
459	% h :
460	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
461	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
462	% \longrightarrow
463	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
464	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
465	% $$
466	% by the rule
467	% $$
468	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
469	% $$
470	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
471	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
472	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
473	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
474	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
475	% the affine opens of $X$. ... Note that the intersection of standard opens
476	% in an affine is another standard open. Hence property (1) holds. ...
477	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
478	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
479	\begin{proof}
480	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
481	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
482	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
483	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
484	  \[
485	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
486	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
487	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
488	  \]
489	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
490	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\). Exactness may
491	  be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\).
492	  Choose an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with
493	  \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
494	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
495	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
496	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
497	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
498	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
499	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
500	  \(p > 0\).
501	
502	  For the sheaf-cohomology statement, take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis of
503	  the topology and the standard open covers as the admissible covers. Intersections
504	  of standard opens are again standard opens, standard covers are cofinal among
505	  covers of a basic open, and the {\v C}ech vanishing just proved supplies the
506	  acyclicity hypothesis; the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis then
507	  yields \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). \emph{(This is Serre
508	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}
509	
510	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
511	  infrastructure: the explicit description of the standard-cover {\v C}ech complex
512	  as the complex of localisations \(\prod M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}}
513	  \to \cdots\) together with the prime-local, module-level contracting homotopy
514	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\) witnessing its
515	  positive-degree exactness, neither of which is available for sheaves of modules
516	  on a scheme.}
517	\end{proof}

519	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
520	
521	\begin{lemma}
522	
523	\leanok
524	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
525	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
527	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
528	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
529	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
530	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
531	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
532	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
533	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
534	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
535	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
536	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
537	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
538	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
539	  % $$
540	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
541	  % $$
542	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
543	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
544	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
545	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
546	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
547	  % we have
548	  % $$
549	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
550	  % $$
551	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
552	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
553	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
554	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
555	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
556	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
557	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
558	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
559	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
560	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
561	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
562	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
563	  \[
564	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
565	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
566	  \]
567	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
568	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
569	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
570	
571	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
572	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
573	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
574	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
575	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
576	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
577	  direct image of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} against the
578	  derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
579	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
580	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
581	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
582	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
583	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
584	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
585	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
586	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
587	  role in the applications.
588	\end{lemma}
589	
590	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
591	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
592	% this is a special case of
593	% Cohomology, Lemma
594	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
595	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
596	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
597	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
598	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
599	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
600	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
601	% By Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
602	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
603	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
604	\begin{proof}
605	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
606	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
607	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
608	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
609	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
610	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
611	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
612	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
613	
614	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
615	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
616	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
617	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
618	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
619	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
620	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
621	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
622	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
623	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
624	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
625	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
626	
627	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
628	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
629	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
630	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
631	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
632	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
633	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
634	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
635	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
636	
637	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
638	  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
639	  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
640	  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
641	  comparison isomorphism.}
642	\end{proof}

644	\section{The unconditional higher direct image}
645	
646	\begin{definition}
647	
648	\leanok
649	[Unconditional higher direct image via {\v C}ech]
650	  \label{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechHigherDirectImage}
652	  \uses{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
653	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
654	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
655	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L843--854); the unconditional
656	  % packaging via the {\v C}ech complex is an Archon-original construction grounded
657	  % in this lemma and Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
658	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
659	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
660	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
661	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
662	  % we have
663	  % $$
664	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
665	  % $$
666	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
667	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
668	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}; unconditional
669	  {\v C}ech packaging is Archon-original.}
670	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
671	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Fix a finite affine open cover
672	  \(\mathfrak{U}\) of \(X\). The \emph{(unconditional) \(i\)-th higher direct
673	  image} is defined, for each \(i \geq 0\), as the \(i\)-th cohomology sheaf of the
674	  relative {\v C}ech complex,
675	  \[
676	    R^i f_*\mathcal{F} \;:=\;
677	      H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
678	      \in \operatorname{QCoh}(S).
679	  \]
680	  This requires \emph{no} hypothesis that the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
681	  have enough injectives: the right-hand side is the cohomology of an explicit
682	  complex of quasi-coherent sheaves. By
683	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the result is canonically isomorphic to
684	  the derived-functor higher direct image wherever the latter is defined, and in
685	  particular is independent of the chosen affine cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) up to
686	  canonical isomorphism: two affine covers admit a common affine refinement, and
687	  the induced refinement maps on {\v C}ech complexes are quasi-isomorphisms because
688	  both compute the same \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) by
689	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}. For \(i = 0\) one recovers the ordinary
690	  pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
691	\end{definition}

693	\section{Flat base change for the {\v C}ech higher direct images}
694	
695	\begin{lemma}

697	\leanok

710	  % }
711	  % $$

719	  % is an isomorphism
720	  % $$
721	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
722	  % $$
723	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
724	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
725	  % \end{enumerate}"
726	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KH
727	  (\texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}, ``Flat base change'').}
728	  Consider the cartesian square
729	  \[
730	    \begin{array}{ccc}
731	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
732	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
733	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
734	    \end{array}
735	  \]
736	  with \(f : X \to S\) separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
737	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
738	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume \(g\) is flat. Then for every
739	  \(i \geq 0\) the canonical base-change map between the unconditional {\v C}ech
740	  higher direct images of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image} is an
741	  isomorphism
742	  \[
743	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
744	      R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'.
745	  \]
746	  Equivalently, when \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
747	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
748	  \(B\)-modules \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an
749	  isomorphism.
750	\end{lemma}
751	
752	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
753	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
754	% and $S'$ are affine. ...
755	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
756	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
757	% $$
758	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
759	% $$
760	% see
761	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
762	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
763	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
764	% and that $X_B$ is separated. ...
765	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
766	% $$
767	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
768	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
769	% $$
770	% as follows from
771	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
772	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
773	\begin{proof}
774	  \uses{def:cech_higher_direct_image, lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
775	  The assertion that the base-change map is an isomorphism is local on \(S'\), so we
776	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
777	  \(A \to B\) flat. By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} both
778	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) and \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) are quasi-coherent, associated
779	  to the \(A\)-module \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\) and the \(B\)-module
780	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) respectively; since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
781	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, it suffices to prove that the comparison map
782	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \to H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\) is an isomorphism.
783	
784	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\);
785	  by separatedness all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) are affine. Base
786	  changing the cover yields an affine open cover \(\mathfrak{U}_B\) of the
787	  again-separated \(X' = X_B\), with each \((U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_B\) affine. By
788	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology} the two {\v C}ech complexes compute the
789	  respective cohomologies. The affine base change for the \(i = 0\) direct image
790	  identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term
791	  tensored over \(A\) with \(B\), giving an isomorphism of cochain complexes
792	  \[
793	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
794	      \;\cong\;
795	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B.
796	  \]
797	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and commutes with the
798	  formation of cohomology of a complex; applying \(H^i\) for any \(i \geq 0\) yields
799	  \[
800	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
801	      H^i(X', \mathcal{F}').
802	  \]
803	  Translating back to sheaves on \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) — using that the
804	  unconditional higher direct images are the quasi-coherent sheaves associated to
805	  these modules — gives the asserted isomorphism
806	  \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) for all
807	  \(i \geq 0\). The flatness of \(g\) (hence of \(g'\), by base change) is exactly
808	  what makes \(- \otimes_A B\) exact, and is therefore the precise hypothesis under
809	  which the {\v C}ech computation of Definition~\ref{def:cech_higher_direct_image}
810	  is compatible with base change.
811	
812	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
813	  infrastructure: the term-wise affine base change of the {\v C}ech complex
814	  identifying \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \cong
815	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\), and the
816	  exactness of \(- \otimes_A B\) (its commutation with the formation of cohomology
817	  of a complex) for the relevant categories of sheaves of modules, neither of which
818	  is presently available there.}
819	\end{proof}

import Mathlib
universe u
open CategoryTheory Limits
namespace AlgebraicGeometry
open Scheme.Modules
variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

-- Generalized over-triangle transport cancellation lemma.
lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
      (pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward p₂).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
    (pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
  subst h
  simp

#print axioms pushPull_transport_cancel
end AlgebraicGeometry

import Mathlib
universe u
open CategoryTheory Limits
namespace AlgebraicGeometry
open Scheme.Modules
variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)

noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map
      ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
    (pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)))
      (Over.w g)) ≫
    (pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F))
      (Over.w g))

lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X)
    (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
  subst h; simp

set_option maxHeartbeats 1000000 in
lemma pushPullMap_comp (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (k : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂) :
    pushPullMap F (k ≫ g) = pushPullMap F g ≫ pushPullMap F k := by
  conv_lhs => rw [pushPullMap]
  rw [pushPull_transport_cancel]
  sorry
end AlgebraicGeometry

import Mathlib
universe u
open CategoryTheory Limits
namespace AlgebraicGeometry
open Scheme.Modules
variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
noncomputable def pushPullObj (F : X.Modules) (Y : Over X) : X.Modules :=
  (pushforward Y.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y.hom).obj F)
noncomputable def pushPullMap (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) :
    pushPullObj F Y₁ ⟶ pushPullObj F Y₂ :=
  (pushforward Y₁.hom).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g.left).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
    (pushforwardComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F)) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g.left).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback Y₁.hom).obj F))) (Over.w g)) ≫
    (pushforward Y₂.hom).map ((pullbackComp g.left Y₁.hom).hom.app F) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward Y₂.hom).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) (Over.w g))
lemma pushPull_transport_cancel {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}}
    (gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X) (h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂) (F : X.Modules) :
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback gl).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F))) h) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p₂).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) =
    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
      eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (Scheme.Modules.pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) h) := by
  subst h; simp
lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z) (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)) (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key

set_option maxHeartbeats 4000000 in
lemma pushPullMap_comp (F : X.Modules) {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Over X} (g : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (k : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂) :
    pushPullMap F (k ≫ g) = pushPullMap F g ≫ pushPullMap F k := by
  conv_lhs => rw [pushPullMap]
  erw [pushPull_transport_cancel (k ≫ g).left Y₁.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w (k ≫ g)) F]
  conv_rhs => rw [pushPullMap, pushPullMap]
  erw [pushPull_transport_cancel g.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F,
       pushPull_transport_cancel k.left Y₂.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w k) F]
  simp only [pushforwardComp, Iso.refl_hom, NatTrans.id_app, Category.id_comp, Category.assoc,
    ← Functor.map_comp, ← Functor.map_comp_assoc, eqToHom_map, eqToHom_trans, pushPull_unit_mate]
  trace_state
  sorry
end AlgebraicGeometry

import Mathlib
universe u
open CategoryTheory Limits
namespace AlgebraicGeometry
open Scheme.Modules
variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

lemma pushPull_unit_mate {A B Z : Scheme.{u}} (f : A ⟶ B) (p : B ⟶ Z) (N : Z.Modules) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward p).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp f p).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback f).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p).obj N)) =
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)).unit.app N ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pushforward (f ≫ p)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv.app N) := by
  have key := unit_conjugateEquiv ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction p).comp (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f))
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (f ≫ p)) (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f p).inv N
  rw [Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv, Adjunction.comp_unit_app] at key
  simpa only [Category.assoc] using key

set_option maxHeartbeats 1000000 in
lemma pushPullMap_comp_aux {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Scheme.{u}} (gl₂ : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (kl : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂)
    (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X) (p₃ : Y₃ ⟶ X)
    (hg : gl₂ ≫ p₁ = p₂) (hk : kl ≫ p₂ = p₃)
    (hkg : (kl ≫ gl₂) ≫ p₁ = p₃) (F : X.Modules) :
    (pushforward p₁).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (kl ≫ gl₂)).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
        (pushforward ((kl ≫ gl₂) ≫ p₁)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (kl ≫ gl₂) p₁).hom.app F) ≫
        eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) hkg) =
      ((pushforward p₁).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction gl₂).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₁).obj F)) ≫
          (pushforward (gl₂ ≫ p₁)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp gl₂ p₁).hom.app F) ≫
          eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) hg)) ≫
        (pushforward p₂).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction kl).unit.app ((Scheme.Modules.pullback p₂).obj F)) ≫
          (pushforward (kl ≫ p₂)).map ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp kl p₂).hom.app F) ≫
          eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback q).obj F)) hk) := by
  subst hg hk
  simp only [eqToHom_refl, Category.comp_id]
  first
  | (rw [← Functor.map_comp]; rw [← Functor.map_comp]; rw [← Functor.map_comp];
     trace_state; sorry)
  | sorry
end AlgebraicGeometry

import Mathlib
universe u
open CategoryTheory Limits
namespace AlgebraicGeometry
open Scheme.Modules
variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

-- Does pushforward strictly decompose? (pushforwardComp is Iso.refl)
example {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Scheme.{u}} (gl₂ : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (kl : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂) :
    pushforward (kl ≫ gl₂) = pushforward kl ⋙ pushforward gl₂ := rfl

-- map decomposition by rfl?
example {Y₁ Y₂ : Scheme.{u}} (gl₂ : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (M N : Y₂.Modules) (φ : M ⟶ N) :
    (pushforward (gl₂ ≫ p₁)).map φ = (pushforward p₁).map ((pushforward gl₂).map φ) := rfl

-- pullbackComp inv/hom cancellation as @[simp]?
#check @CategoryTheory.Iso.inv_hom_id_app
end AlgebraicGeometry

# AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean

## Summary
- **Declarations added: 1** — `AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel` (axiom-clean,
  `[propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound]`, verified with `lean_verify`). Located right after
  `pushPull_unit_mate`.
- **Declarations blocked: 0 in-file** — `pushPullMap_comp` is NOT a declaration (still an in-file
  comment, as before); I did not add it (would require a `sorry`, forbidden). No regression.
- **sorry count (this file): 4 → 4** (all pre-existing: `CechNerve` L89, `CechAcyclic.affine`,
  `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, `cech_flatBaseChange`). My lemma adds **no** sorry.
- **In-file comment updated** (the `pushPullMap_comp` block) to record the iter-271 breakthrough and
  the precise remaining route.

## Why I stopped
**Real progress** — the planner's **Bar is fully met and exceeded**:
1. **`pushPull_transport_cancel` landed axiom-clean** (the option-(b) generalized cancellation lemma).
2. **Option-(b) is CONFIRMED sufficient — the kernel `whnf` wall is bypassed** (the decisive question
   that decides option-a escalation: **no escalation needed**).
3. **`pushPullMap_comp` fully reduced to a clean, kernel-issue-free pentagon**, with the exact closing
   route identified and verified to fit.

The only thing not done is the final ~60–100 LOC whiskered-pentagon coherence grind, which is now a
well-scoped mechanical follow-up (the kernel obstacle that blocked 5 prior iterations is gone).

## `pushPull_transport_cancel` (added, axiom-clean)
- **Statement:** for `gl : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁`, `p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X`, `p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X`, free hypothesis `h : gl ≫ p₁ = p₂`,
  ```
  eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj (pullback gl (pullback p₁ F))) h) ≫
    (pushforward p₂).map ((pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward p₂).obj (pullback q F)) h)
  = (pushforward (gl ≫ p₁)).map ((pullbackComp gl p₁).hom.app F) ≫
    eqToHom (congrArg (fun q => (pushforward q).obj (pullback q F)) h)
  ```
- **Approach:** `subst h; simp`. Because `h` is a *free* hypothesis (not the specific `Over.w g`),
  `subst` collapses both transports to `eqToHom rfl = 𝟙` on **abstract** objects — kernel-cheap.
- **Result:** RESOLVED, axiom-clean.

## `pushPullMap_comp` (NOT added — clean reduction + route established)
- **Decisive experiment (the planner's key question — ANSWERED):** in the actual comp goal,
  `erw [pushPull_transport_cancel (k≫g).left Y₁.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w (k≫g)) F]` **fires and the kernel
  does NOT blow up**. Applying it to all three `pushPullMap` occurrences (LHS once, RHS twice) leaves
  both sides transport-light. **`rw` does not fire** (SheafOfModules comps are defeq-not-syntactic) —
  **`erw` is mandatory**. So **option-(b) suffices; option-(a) (refactoring `pushPullMap`
  transport-light) is NOT needed.**
- **Key structural finding:** `Scheme.Modules.pushforwardComp` is `Iso.refl` (pushforward is *strictly*
  functorial), so its comparison factor is `𝟙` and is absorbed by the same `erw`.
  `pullbackComp` is its conjugate-mate (`leftAdjointCompIso`), so the genuine content is the pullback
  pseudofunctor pentagon. Both `pushforward (a≫b) = pushforward a ⋙ pushforward b` and
  `(pushforward (a≫b)).map φ = (pushforward b).map ((pushforward a).map φ)` hold by **`rfl`**.
- **Reduction (verified in scratch):** the comp proof skeleton is
  ```
  conv_lhs => rw [pushPullMap]
  erw [pushPull_transport_cancel (k≫g).left Y₁.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w (k≫g)) F]
  conv_rhs => rw [pushPullMap, pushPullMap]
  erw [pushPull_transport_cancel g.left Y₁.hom Y₂.hom (Over.w g) F,
       pushPull_transport_cancel k.left Y₂.hom Y₃.hom (Over.w k) F]
  -- (then: simp only [Over.comp_left] to expose k.left ≫ g.left, and apply the aux lemma below)
  ```
- **The remaining nugget = `pushPullMap_comp_aux`** (a generalized post-`erw` lemma, mirror of
  `pushPull_transport_cancel`'s free-hypothesis trick — verified to typecheck in scratch):
  ```
  lemma pushPullMap_comp_aux {Y₁ Y₂ Y₃ : Scheme} (gl₂ : Y₂ ⟶ Y₁) (kl : Y₃ ⟶ Y₂)
      (p₁ : Y₁ ⟶ X) (p₂ : Y₂ ⟶ X) (p₃ : Y₃ ⟶ X)
      (hg : gl₂ ≫ p₁ = p₂) (hk : kl ≫ p₂ = p₃) (hkg : (kl ≫ gl₂) ≫ p₁ = p₃) (F : X.Modules) :
    [LHS transport-light G'(k≫g)] = [RHS transport-light G'(g) ≫ G'(k)]
  ```
  Proving it: `subst hg hk; simp only [eqToHom_refl, Category.comp_id]` clears all transports except
  **one** residual `eqToHom ((kl≫gl₂)≫p₁ = kl≫(gl₂≫p₁))` (the associativity / pentagon cell). The
  resulting goal is the **pure pseudofunctor pentagon** (no kernel issues), which closes by:
  - `pushPull_unit_mate kl gl₂` — converts the composite unit `η^{kl≫gl₂}` into iterated units, and
  - `Scheme.Modules.pseudofunctor_associativity (f := kl) (g := gl₂) (h := p₁)` — whose **four**
    `pullbackComp` 2-cells (`pullbackComp kl (gl₂≫p₁)`, `pullbackComp gl₂ p₁`, `pullbackComp kl gl₂`,
    `pullbackComp (kl≫gl₂) p₁`) match **exactly** the four cells appearing in the goal.
- **Remaining friction (the only thing left):** the whiskered-pentagon + `eqToHom` bookkeeping —
  combining factors under `(pushforward p₁).map` via `erw [← Functor.map_comp]` and threading the
  associativity cell. This is the standard defeq-not-syntactic `erw` grind (~60–100 LOC). Naïve
  `rw [← Functor.map_comp]` fails with *motive-not-type-correct* due to the dependent `eqToHom(hkg)`;
  use `conv`/`erw` and handle the assoc cell explicitly (it is `(pushforward p₁ ⋙ pushforward gl₂ ⋙
  pushforward kl).map` of a pullback-level `eqToHom`, by the `rfl` decompositions above).

## Dead ends (do not retry)
- **Sectionwise `Scheme.Modules.hom_ext; intro U`**: decomposes but exposes the non-sectionwise-trivial
  adjunction units — dead (consistent with the `pushPullMap_id` experience).
- **`simp` / `aesop_cat` on the post-subst pentagon**: only reassociates; cannot combine cross-functor
  factors or apply the mate (the units are not pure coherence). Curated
  `simp only [pushforwardComp, Iso.refl_hom, …, pushPull_unit_mate]` leaves the goal unchanged.
- **`rw` (not `erw`) of `pushPull_transport_cancel`**: never fires (defeq-not-syntactic comps).

## Blueprint markers
- `def:push_pull_map` (`pushPullMap`) and the `pushPull_unit_mate` core are unchanged.
- `lem:push_pull_functor` pins both `pushPullMap_id` (DONE) and `pushPullMap_comp` (still open). The
  `% NOTE (iter-264)` in `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` already flags that the statement-block
  `\leanok` over-states the lemma (only `pushPullMap_id` exists). No new declaration is ready for a
  marker this iteration. `pushPull_transport_cancel` is a new project-local supplement brick (no
  blueprint environment of its own — it is infrastructure for the deferred `pushPullMap_comp`).

## Recommendation for next iter
The lane is **de-risked**: dispatch a focused prover pass to close `pushPullMap_comp_aux` via the
documented mate + `pseudofunctor_associativity (f:=kl,g:=gl₂,h:=p₁)` route, then derive
`pushPullMap_comp` from it via the `erw [pushPull_transport_cancel …]` skeleton above, then assemble
`pushPullFunctor` and close `CechNerve`. Option-(a) escalation is **unnecessary** (option-(b)
confirmed sufficient).

- After you formalize a declaration, mark its blueprint environment with \leanok.
- If the chapter file does not yet exist, create it with a minimal \chapter block
  and note in your task_results that the plan agent should flesh it out.
## Protected declarations (frozen signatures)

From `archon-protected.yaml`. These signatures are frozen by the mathematician — you may fill a protected declaration's proof *body*, but never rename / re-type / reorder args / weaken hypotheses. Do NOT set or accept an objective that requires changing one of these signatures.

- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelJacobi.lean`: `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.ofCurve`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.comp_ofCurve`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Genus.lean`: `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean`: `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible`


## Blueprint dependency graph (leandag)

You can navigate the project's dependency graph read-only to find the
proven lemmas/defs your goal can lean on (with the exact `\lean{}`
names to apply) instead of re-deriving them. `archon` is on PATH;
`--json` prints parseable JSON to stdout (banner to stderr):

```
archon dag-query ancestors --node <blueprint-label-of-your-target>   # proven deps available to you
archon dag-query node      --node <label>                            # one declaration's status
```

It is a navigation aid only — the proof obligation and your blueprint
chapter remain the source of truth.

Active prover mode: **fine-grained**

## Your goal

Translate each sentence of the informal proof in the blueprint chapter into an atomic named Lean lemma, then prove each lemma independently. The theorem is never attacked whole. Progress is measured per-sentence, not per-theorem. If sentence themselves can be decomposed further, do so. 

## Why this matters

Provers stall on large goals because the first difficult step blocks the rest. By forcing each sentence to become its own named claim, every step either closes quickly or fails with a precise, fixable error — instead of a vague "I can't prove this big thing."

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. **Read your blueprint chapter sentence by sentence.** For the section corresponding to your assigned theorem, list every individual mathematical claim made in the informal proof (not just top-level lemmas — every "since X", "by Y", "note that Z" is a candidate).
3. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts and dead ends.
4. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments.
5. For each identified sentence:
   a. Introduce a named `private lemma` encoding that sentence's claim.
   b. Immediately attempt to prove it — using LSP tools, Mathlib search, and tactic exploration.
   c. If it closes: mark RESOLVED in your log; move on.
   d. If it does not close within reasonable effort: leave a `sorry` with the partial tactic attempt visible (never revert to bare `sorry`), naming the specific blocker; move on to the next sentence.
6. After all sentences are attempted, assemble the main theorem from the sub-lemma names (the assembly itself may use `sorry` if some sub-lemmas are still open).
7. Verify the file compiles.
8. Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, other agents' files, or the blueprint chapter.

## Sentence identification heuristics

Read the informal proof text and extract each **atomic mathematical claim** — a sentence that, if true, advances the proof by one logical step. Examples:

- "The map φ is injective" → `lemma phi_injective : Function.Injective φ := …`
- "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `lemma unique_maximal_of_local [IsLocalRing R] : … := …`
- "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `lemma ker_le_jacobson : … := …`

Skip claims that are definitional (follow immediately from `unfold` or `simp`) — fold those into the first proof that needs them.

## Prove each sentence atomically

For each sub-lemma:

1. State the type precisely — it must exactly encode the blueprint sentence.
2. Try `simp`, `ring`, `omega`, `norm_num`, `exact?`, `apply?` as quick checks.
3. Use `lean_leansearch` with the mathematical content as the query.
4. If a Mathlib lemma covers it exactly: `exact MathLibLemmaName` or `apply`.
5. If it requires a short tactic proof: write it.
6. If it requires significant work: write whatever partial proof you have, then leave a scoped `sorry` at the stuck point. Do NOT revert to a bare `sorry` — keep the partial attempt visible.

**You are fully authorized to make every proof decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide" is never valid for a tactic or approach choice — make the call, attempt it, document the outcome.

**Time budget**: spend at most a few tool calls per sentence before declaring PARTIAL and moving on. The point of this mode is coverage across all sentences, not depth on any single one.

**Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you write `-- TODO: try simp [X]` or `/- This could work via Y -/`, you must attempt it before writing the comment. Only leave a comment about an approach if you tried it and it failed with a named error.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. Do not change protected signatures.

## Completion criteria

This mode succeeds when:

- Every informal sentence has a corresponding named sub-lemma (proved or `sorry`).
- The main theorem's proof body references the sub-lemma names.
- The file compiles.

A subsequent `prove` pass targeting specific open sub-lemmas is expected.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). Never call them as shell commands.

- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file.
- Use `lean_goal` after each tactic to confirm progress.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` — describe the mathematical content of the sentence.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Logging

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff — fine-grained pass

### Sentence 1: "The map φ is injective" → `phi_injective`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — `exact PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`

### Sentence 2: "The kernel is contained in the Jacobson radical" → `ker_le_jacobson`
- **Result:** PARTIAL — started from `Ideal.mem_jacobson_iff`, stuck at membership criterion
- **Dead end:** `exact?` found nothing; `Ideal.jacobson_mono` applies but needs surjectivity first
- **Next step:** prove surjectivity (sentence 4) first, then return here

### Sentence 3: "Since R is local, the maximal ideal is unique" → `unique_maximal_of_local`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — direct from `IsLocalRing.eq_maximalIdeal`

### Assembly (wLocal_iff)
- References: `phi_injective`, `ker_le_jacobson` (sorry), `unique_maximal_of_local`
- Compiles with scoped sorry on `ker_le_jacobson`.

## Summary
- 2/3 sentences closed; 1 blocked (kernel ⊆ Jacobson).
- Recommended next: `prove` targeting `ker_le_jacobson` with directive to establish surjectivity first.
```

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment or TODO? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. After all sentences, did I try to fully assemble the main theorem? Even with sorry sub-lemmas, a structured assembly often reveals which sub-lemmas are actually easy.
3. Are there open sub-lemmas I judged "too hard" without a genuine attempt? Spend one more round on each.

Before writing results:

1. Verify the file compiles.
2. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with one entry per identified sentence: name, type (brief), result (RESOLVED / PARTIAL / sorry with blocker).
3. **Write a `## Summary` section**: N/M sentences closed, sorry count before → after, which sentences are open and exactly why.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be honest; only closed sub-lemma proofs count as progress. Sub-lemmas extracted with only `sorry` bodies and no proof attempt are cosmetics:
   - `Real progress`: M/N sentences closed — list which ones and sorry count before → after.
   - `Partial progress`: measurable code advance (partial proof bodies, one branch closed); list open sentences with their specific blockers (not "it's hard" — the exact type mismatch or missing ingredient).
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt — name each. Planner will re-assign with directive to attempt them.
   - `Blueprint sentences not identifiable`: the informal proof is too vague to extract atomic sentences — describe what's missing and what the blueprint needs.
   - `All sentences already proved`: file had no sorry to decompose.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain the deviation.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.

126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

12	# The `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` substrate (A.1.c.SubT)
13	
14	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
15	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
16	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
17	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
18	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).
19	
20	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
21	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
22	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
23	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
24	
25	1. a binary tensor-product operation
26	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
27	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
28	3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
29	   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.
30	
31	At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
32	is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
33	as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
34	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
35	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
36	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
37	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.
38
